image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |
raw_comment: I have a question. Why do people think we should limit bodies that are not our own? Why do men get to decide what my body will do or look like? When you are born, I guess someone has already decided what your life will be like. people need to travel thousands of miles to save their life. Does that sound fair?

raw_comment: What I noticed is that there is a clear trend of which where these states are and their policies of abortion and sex education. Most of the states for example, Georgia, Texas, Kentucky, etc, are in the south and have abortion either banned or will be banned and have a heavy push for abstinence in their sex education. Even some liberal states have abstinences pushed in their education such as Oregon and Washington, but, abortion is available when needed. The biggest surprise to me in this graph is Virginia. They don’t have abortion banned or ready to be banned nor do they push for abstinences. What I wonder is the root cause for why these states are. For most they are self explanatory, they were in the south er go they are conservative, but some continue to wonder me. This affects my community because as a whole, we in the United States should stick together in manners of issues such as this. If I could ad a headline to this graph it would be “what does your state say about sex education?”

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |

What do you notice?
From looking at this graph, I notice that in most of the southern and western states, abortion is heavily restricted or banned. I also noticed that the states that stressed abstinence in school also have strict abortion laws. I also noticed that in most of the northeastern states, abortion is largely available.

From looking at this graph, I wonder what factors contribute to states choosing to have strict abortions laws or relaxed abortion laws. Is it religion or the population?

raw_comment:
What's going on in this graph
From this graph, I noticed that the states who are already orange and have restricted the right to abortion access have the biggest emphasis on teaching abstinence in school. These states are emphasizing the importance of sexual safety yet contrast it by restricting the right to get abortions if needed. Texas specifically, has the highest number of women of reproductive age yet heavily emphasizes the restriction of pregnancy termination. 

what do I wonder
I wonder why almost every single box in orange that restricts abortion also has a black line for stressed sex education. Yet the grey boxes vary, some stressing education and others not. What about these states, despite having the same people and circumstances, makes them decide to restrict something so beneficial?

how does this relate to me and my community?
This relates to me and my community because first of all, as a woman it is important to know where my rights to an abortion are restricted. Second of all, this relates to my community because living in new jersey I know I have access to my reproductive rights.

raw_comment:
I notice that Texas seems to be the the state with the largest amount of women of reproductive age where abortion is heavily restricted or banned. Meanwhile California, with 8 million women of reproductive age, has abortion largely available. I also notice that in a majority of the states where abortion is heavily restricted, it is required that abstinence is stressed and taught heavily in sex education. 
I wonder what party dominates these states, and whether they are more republican or democratic. I also wonder when the "reproductive age" begins and ends. I also wonder how much the stressing of abstinence in sex education effects a state's view of abortion
This topic is very important to my community because as I go to an all girls school, it is important to see and acknowledge where our right to have an abortion is available, and where it is not. It is very eye opening to see how many states are orange.

raw_comment:
I noticed that most of the Eastern Coast states have abortion largely available, but have abstinence stressed in sex education. While in the Western states abortion is largely available, but has abstinence stressed as well and I would say there are more states that heavily restrict or banned abortion. Also, almost all the Southern states restrict or banned abortion while stressing abstinence in sex education. Looking at the graph you are able to see how the country divides in half when it comes to having abortion restricted or banned. 

I wonder if the country being divided almost in half could be because of different political views in each state. I wonder if the states that currently restrict/ban abortion could possibly change as younger people with a more open mind start voting. Also I wonder if abstinence is being taught in sex ed. in most states how does it affect the number of abortions, is there a change in anyway comparing a state who teaches it or not. 

This relates to me and my community because in New Jersey where I'm from abstinence is taught and abortions are available, but many states and women of reproductive age don't have it available, which could be unfair. Many people have fought to have control over their own bodies, but a lot of states restrict the choice to do so.

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |
raw_comment:
What do you notice? 
I notice that the majority of the states where abortion is heavily restricted, banned, or will be soon banned require abstinence to be taught in sexual education. I also notice that women of reproductive age are ages 15 to 44. But how can a 15-year-old who is still in high school be considered a woman or an adult? In the graph, Texas and Florida have a bigger size due to having a higher number of women of reproductive age. These states have abortion banned which means that adolescents 15-19 don't have access to abortion. 

What do you wonder?
I wonder if the states that teach abstinence have a lower or higher number of pregnancies than the states that don't teach abstinence. I  wonder if there are any differences in the topics that are taught in the states that allow abortions compared to those that have a ban or restriction on abortion.  I also wonder if Roe v. Wade being overturned will change this graph in the future and will lead to more states banning abortions. 

How does this relate to you and your community?
This relates to me and my community because I live in New Jersey where abortion is available and is an option for women. Knowing that women in other states don't have the option to have an abortion is very disappointing and saddening. As women, we should have control over our bodies. 

What’s going on in this graph?

Abstinence In Sex Education and Women's Reproductive Rights

raw_comment: Well, obviously, this shows that most states that restrict or ban abortion also require that abstinence be stressed in sex education. Expecting kids whose brains aren't fully formed and who are vulnerable to peer pressure to practice abstinence is unrealistic. Unless these states are providing easy access to other methods of birth control and educating students about them, they're contributing to the situations that create a demand for the abortions they won't provide. What their ultimate goal is baffles me.

raw_comment:
I notice a lot of variability among the states and their laws concerning size, sex education, and legality of abortion. States that are typically liberal, such as California and New York, have large populations of women of reproductive age and allow abortion without stressing abstinence in sex ed. Typically conservative states like Texas and Georgia restrict or ban abortion and require abstinence to be stressed in sex ed. I notice that states with large populations of women of reproductive age aren’t just one way or another. For example, California and Texas are the two largest squared with completely different laws and rules.

I wonder how these squares will change over time? Which squares will increase in size, and which will decrease in size? How will new laws on sex ed change what is taught and what isn’t taught? Will some states legalize abortion or make stricter restrictions? 

This applies to me because I am a woman of reproductive age, and I need to know the laws within my state. 

My headline is: “Boxed in: How the recent overturn of Roe v. Wade changed reproductive rights across America”

raw_comment:
Thanks to all the students who responded. Here are some of the catchy headlines that we received: “Gaps In Sexual Education May Go Hand In Hand With Abortion Access” by ViVi of Eden Prairie High School in Minnesota; “America Re-Shaped: A Look at the Association Between Abstinence Education and Abortion Access” by Ben of The Lib; “From Abstinence Education to Abortion Restrictions: Are We Going Backwards?” by Jessemberly of Perth Amboy High School in New Jersey; “The Emphasis of Abstinence Will Not Stop Abortion” by Charlotte of Academy of Saint Elizabeth in Morristown, New Jersey; and  “Which States Promote Abstinence Over Providing Access to Abortions?” by Hannah of California Polytechnic San Luis Obispo.

You can continue to post responses. Please check back by Friday, April 28 to see the Reveal with the free link to the original New York Times article, some questions about this graph and a few Stat Nuggets.

The last graph of the school year will be released on April 28 and moderated on Wednesday, May 5. The topic – baseball. 

Signing off – Sharon Hessney from Boston, who lives in a state that does not restrict abortion and does not require that abstinence be stressed in sex education, and Jenny Argyle, maths and statistics teacher at the Royal Grammar School in Newcastle-upon-Tyne, United Kingdom, who lives in a country that has no restrictions around abortion and does not require that abstinence be stressed in sex education.

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |
raw_comment:

2. I wonder why some states stress abstinence, while others who just have little restrictions, do not. Is it because maybe some states have stronger religious ties in their history? 
3. This relates to my community and I as abortion is a hot topic right now in the country/among people my age, also many states are changing and or strengthening their rules on abortion. 
4. Ban by Block: States Rules on Sex Education and Abortion

raw_comment:
1. I noticed that some of the states where abortion is heavily restricted, abstinence is not being taught in sex education. I also noticed that its prominently the southern states that restrict abortion. I've seen that the states with the strongest abortion restrictions or outright bans are generally ones where sex education stresses abstinence. Abortion is more readily available in states that do not encourage abstinence. I also see that Texas is the only state (along with California and New York) that prohibits abortions and requires abstinence education. Few states outlaw or restrict abortion and place little emphasis on abstinence.
2. Based on this graph, I'm wondering if there's a link between sexual education in schools and abortion. Is it true, for example, that the more abstinence is taught, the fewer abortions there are, and vice versa? Or our decisions are influenced less by what we learn in school and more by the circumstances we choose to surround ourselves with. 
3. Although abstinence is strongly advocated in schools in New Jersey, abortion is available. NJ is also close to other states where abortion is legal. Also, people from states that abortion is restricted, travel to states like NJ in order to get an abortion safely. Our state has given women the privilege to choose what they wish to do with their body.
4. Is It Up To The State or The Women?

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |

And although the school's curriculum is to teach abstinence, I think rather than doing this, they should be teaching more about safe sex. image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |
raw_comment:
1. I noticed that most states that have heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon are the same states that Abstinence is required to be taught in sex education. The states where abortion is highly available have more liberal sex education where abstinence isn't stressed as much. Also, I've noticed that the states that are more down south have stricter abortion laws like Texas and Florida.

2. I wonder why we can't have Abstinence being stressed in sex education and abortion being largely available. Like Washington, New Jersey, North Carolina, etc. Those states can do it so why can't we all? Taking a woman's right of choice is not okay if it's the children you are worried about getting a better education in that area. 

3. In my community I actually volunteer in sex education. I'm from New Jersey and during the presentations we stress Abstinence however we have the ability to also say where clinics are if something does happen and get to tell them that they do have a choice!

4. Abstinence-Only Education: Is Taking Our Choice Away Necessary?

raw_comment:
I noticed that this graph separates states which have strict abortion laws and from those don't. It additionally shows the number of women in every state that are pregnant. California, with 8 million women, has the most women of reproductive age than all the other states. In California, abortion is very accessible. I also noticed that Repubian have stricter abortion laws. While states that are strongly against abortion are mainly democratic states away from the South.

I wonder how sex education is being taught and how contraceptives are being incentivised. I wonder how many of these states make schools teach sex education and how many don't. I also wonder the extent to which religion influences these states and if it changes the way they teach sex ed.

This relates to me and my community because where I live abortion isn't restricted. But sex education is still mandated to be taught. I wonder if there is any connection between the two in my community. It affects me because many people I know have gotten pregnant. This might be because of the way sex ed is taught.

Abstinence and abortion, how sex ed is affecting America.

raw_comment:
1. Liberals typically live in the northeast and California, where there are glaringly no laws governing abortion or abstinence. The bulk of the states with restrictions on abortion that also mandate abstinence education in schools is from the South or the Midwest, showing that attitudes on abortion are partisan, with more conservative states being more opposed to it.
2. I wonder why some states do not include abstinence in their curricula even though they forbid abortion. I also wonder how much emphasis is placed on abstinence in these jurisdictions' regular instruction. What bearing does a state's or region's culture have on the choice?
3. This is important to my community because abortion is widely available and accepted despite the fact that abstinence is encouraged in sex education where I reside in New Jersey. When a kid is unexpected and the parents are unprepared to be parents, abortion is a necessary practice that gives women the freedom to utilize their bodies anyway they see fit. These new abortion laws, which are a result of the Roe v. Wade ruling, can tempt people to move to a state where abortion is legal.
4. "Roe v. Wade: Abortion vs America"

raw_comment:
1. When I look at the graph I saw the majority of the states that have restricted or banned abortion are found in the South. Abstinence is emphasized in the majority of states, albeit it is largely centered in middle America and the South. The majority of the states with restrictions that also call for abstinence education in schools are from the South or the Midwest, showing that opinions on abortion are divided along political lines, with more conservative states opposing abortion.
2. As more young people who will be voting in the future adopt pro-choice and pro-abortion positions, I wonder how this graph will evolve over time. This might result in a significant change in the laws that many states currently have in place. I wonder if the teaching from back in the day is what is affecting people's choices whether they're pro-life or pro-choice. 
3. This is relevant to my community because there has been a strong push in recent years to legislate against the autonomy of those who are able to give birth, and I see a sense of urgency in those around me to grant people the right to control their own bodies because we are all impacted by the choices our legislators make. Abstinence is encouraged in sex education where I reside in New Jersey, but abortion is freely accessible and accepted. These new abortion laws can tempt people to move to a state where abortion is legal.
4. Our Bodies but, Our States Choice

2. I wonder why these sections have the laws they do.  The South seems to be seen as more right leaning, the north seems to be more left.  Does this illustrate each political side's views on abortion, and how right-wingers aren't in favor for abortion while leftists are.

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |
raw_comment:
1. I've noticed that the states with the strongest abortion restrictions or outright bans are generally ones where sex education stresses abstinence. Abortion is more readily available in states that do not encourage abstinence. I also see that Texas is the only state (along with California and New York) that prohibits abortions and requires abstinence education. Few states outlaw or restrict abortion and place little emphasis on abstinence. 

2. This graph makes me question if there is a link between the sexual education given in schools and abortion. Is it true, for example, that the more abstinence is taught, the fewer abortions there are, and vice versa? Or our decisions are influenced less by what we learn in school and more by the circumstances we choose to surround ourselves with. 

3. As someone who resides in New Jersey I can see the influence sexual education has on students, and how it can relates to the abortion rates in the state

4. "The Effect Sexual Education Has On Abortion Rates in the US"

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |
raw_comment:
1. I notice a lot of states where abortion is heavily restricted or banned or likely will be soon located in the south of the U.S. As well as many of the states stress abstinence in sex education even if abortion is not banned. Also, most of the states that have banned abortion also are states that are on the Republican side of politics. 
2. I wonder if all of these states have banned abortion completely or if there are some exceptions. I also wonder, if there are no exceptions, if there are different consequences depending on the circumstances that lead the person to have an abortion.
3. This relates to me and my community because New Jersey currently has not put a ban on abortion and it is available to so many in the state. Abstinence is taught in school, unfortunately, however, this tends to cause safe sex to be spoken less about when both should be explained equally. 
4. Pro-Life vs Pro-Choice: U.S Split On Abortion Ban

raw_comment: This graph demonstrates the various correlations between states who advocate for abstinence or not and whether they restrict abortion or not.  One of the main things that stands out is how states like California and New York do not have heavy restrictions on abortion, but also do not stress abstinence.  On the other hand, states like Texas and Florida both restrict abortion and require abstinence to be a part of sex education curriculum.  One of the main trends is how the southern states have the higher restrictions as well as stricter enforcement of abstinence teachings, whereas the northern states do not.  It also appears that the states who restrict abortion in comparison to those who do not is rather even.  I wonder how this chart would have looked 50 years ago when the Roe v. Wade decision came out.  Would there be more states with restrictions?  Would states emphasize abstinence more?  I also wonder how politics come into play with predicting which states will soon restrict or ban abortion.  This graph is relevant to me and my community because abortion is an extremely controversial subject in politics and people feel very passionately about their opinions.  I would entitle this graph: "Abstinence v. Abortion."

raw_comment:
what I noticed 

I noticed that this graph reveals which states in the US have strict laws on abortion and which don't. In addition, it shows the number of women in every state that are pregnant. California, with 8 million women, has the most women of reproductive age than every other state. In California, abortion is very accessible. I also noticed that abortion is very restricted in several states down south, which are known to be more Republican. While states that are strongly against abortion are mainly democratic states.

what I wonder 

I'm curious about why states that restrict abortion wouldn't promote abstinence meanwhile states that support the right to abortion do. I also wonder what topics are taught in different schools where abstinence is not a priority and how their school systems compare to those in various places around the nation. 

How it relates to my community 

This relates to me and those around me since abortions are easily accessible in New Jersey. However, schools are required to teach abstinence, which I disagree with because instead, they could be teaching about safe sex. I strongly believe that everyone has the right to do what they want with their bodies. 

catchy title 

Their body Their state's choice

raw_comment:
The first thing I noticed was how many more states than I had anticipated have abortion bans. For some reason, I thought that there were only a few states that did this. I'm curious to see how the current proportion of orange states compares to what it would have been five or ten years ago.

I noticed that New Jersey is one of the states outlined in black, meaning that abstinence is required to be taught in sex Ed. Out of the states pictured more than 10 of them are outlined in black.

When I was taught sex Education we were shown different forms of sexual safety, but the one truly stressed the most was abstinence. We were taught that the only assured way of not getting STD’s or STI’s was through abstinence as well as stopping a pregnancy. I liked the transparency that we got, but it also felt like it was a rule that they had to teach it that way and that the teachers might’ve wanted to approach it differently.

Sexual Education and Abortion Restrictions, is Abstinence the only way?

## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |
raw_comment:
This graph depicts the abortion accessibility across the United States, with the orange representing the states where abortion is restricted or banned now or in the future, and the gray representing the states where abortion is largely available.I noticed that approximately half the states have already banned abortion or will ban it soon. 

I wonder how extensive the sex education is regarding contraceptives in these states as well. I wonder how many of these states mandate sex education at all. I also wonder the extent to which religion influences these states and their policies toward abortion and sex education in general.

I observed that nearly all of the states who have restricted abortions also emphasize abstenince. I also noticed that a majority of the states that had banned abortion are republican. A majority of these states are part of the Bible Belt, which is considered to a predominantly Christian center of the United States. Thus, the focus on abstinence and restriction of abortion could be related to religion.

This relates to me and my community because where I live abortion is still widely available but abstinence is required to be stressed in sex education. Abortion is an important issue for all of us to maintain rights over our bodies. I am concerned that in states that focus on abstinence and ban abortion will result in more teen pregnancies. 

Headline:
A Map Of America: Abortion Restrictions and Abstinence Requirements

raw_comment:
I noticed that this graph shows in which US states abortion is heavily restricted or widely available. It also shows the number of women in each state of reproductive age. The state with the largest number of women of reproductive age is California, with 8 million women. Abortion is widely available in California. I also noticed that in many states in the South, abortion is heavily restricted; these states are known to vote Republican. In contrast, the states where abortion is widely available are known to vote Democrat. 

I wonder how widely available sex education is in each state, not just sex education emphasizing abstinence. I also wonder if sex education is biased in certain states. I wonder if the states that have heavy restrictions have good sex education programs or not. 

This relates to me because I am a woman living in New Jersey. Although abortion is widely available in my state, it is not available in many other states. Many of the restrictions placed on abortion are actually dangerous to the life of the pregnant individual, in cases of ectopic pregnancy or high risk pregnancies abortions are done to save the mother’s life.  These restrictions are made by elected officials who are the majority of are men. They do not take into account the voice of people who are actually affected by these restrictions. 

Headline: Sex Education and its Connection to Abortion Restrictions

raw_comment:
What do you notice?
I noticed that out of all 50 states, 21 of them are marked orange meaning abortion is heavily restricted or likely will be in almost half of the entire country. I also noticed that most of the states marked orange were overall smaller squares than the ones who weren’t marked orange. So mostly states with less women of reproductive age are the states where abortion is being restricted. I also noticed that most of the states restricting abortion are the states to the south.

What do you wonder?
I wonder why most of the states that are restricting abortions are the ones with less women at a reproductive age and why the amount of states restricting abortion are much higher in the south than the north. I also wonder why this data was represented this way with squares rather than some other kind of graph.

How does this relate to you and your community?
This relates to my community and I because it informs us about what’s happening in the country and how we may be affected. Although New Jersey isn’t colored orange, the graph itself can give us insight and let us know that eventually the changes that are happening in other states may be incorporated into where we live as well.

raw_comment:
I first noticed that there are much more states banning abortion than I would have thought. For some reason, I assumed that there were just a handful of states where this was the case. I wonder how the number of orange states now compares to the number that would have been orange 5 or 10 years ago.

I also noticed that my home state of Washington is one of the states where abstinence is required to be stressed in sex education. This was interesting to me because it certainly never seemed that abstinence was "stressed" in our sex education at school. I wonder what the extent of this requirement is. It seems like something that would be hard to enforce. I would be curious what the actual writing is of the law that requires this because "stressed in sex education" is vague. 

Another thing that I noticed is that while there does appear to be a relationship between abstinence-education states and anti-abortion states, the population size of states has no relationship with either.

This graph is relevant to me because I have always lived in areas where abortion is non restricted and readily available. Also areas that are progressive in terms of sexual education. Because of this, I often forget about the differing views and laws of other places in the country and don't think about how this can have such a large effect on peoples lives.

I noticed that southern states have more restrictions on abortion. I also noticed that in these states, abstinence is stressed in sex education. In contrast, northern states tend to have less restrictions on abortion, as well as having sex education classes that do not stress the importance of abstinence. Is there a correlation between these factors and the fact that most southern states are republican and vice versa? California and New Jersey, for instance, have a large left-leaning population and they strongly favor abortion. I also wonder how education policy plays into this topic of abortion?
This relates to me and my community because as a woman, I have seen some of my own peers become affected by the changes in legislation following the overturning of Roe v. Wade.

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |

raw_comment:
1: The majority of the states with heavy abortion restrictions also have abstinence required for sexual education. 17 of 21 states with heavy abortion restrictions also require abstinence to be taught. Along with that, a lot of the states with heavy abortion restrictions are around each other geographically. They’re mostly in the Midwest to the south. They also mostly have similar-sized populations being almost directly in the middle. 

2: I wonder why Wyoming has so few people in such a big state, I wonder how this chart will change over the years, I wonder why some schools don’t require abstinence in sex education, I wonder which color has more people overall, I wonder how the women in the “orange” states feel about their state's laws, I wonder if those women wish they get more sex education or not

3: This topic heavily impacts our community as our group has two girls in it. Abortion access is important for us to continue to have choices over our bodies. Not only that, but as students what we’re taught in sex ed can change a lot of things for us. If states are focusing on abstinence and not other forms of birth control, teen pregnancy may go up. With no options, we may have more teen parents. This could cause a lot of abnormalities in the United States economy and workforce. 

4: “Gaps In Sexual Education May Go Hand In Hand With Abortion Access”

raw_comment:
A striking trend of this graph is that most states that are pro-life are Republican-controlled, mostly Midwest/Southern states. Besides that, the vast majority of states that ban abortion also stress it in sex education, with 4 outliers (the Dakotas, Wyoming, and West Virginia). This reflects the pro-life stance of Republicans, which also means that people in Southern states who want abortion would either need to illegally ship drugs in or trek far away to northeastern states to get an abortion. This would probably discourage many Southerners to get abortions, even if they need them to keep their financial stability.
“The Pro-Life Divide” is clearly shown in this graph, and makes a fitting title.

Welcome to Wednesday's live moderation of “What’s Going On in This Graph?”  

Today’s map, published on  July 7, 223, shows which states restrict abortion and which states require abstinence to be stressed in sex education classes.  Please read the notes below the map about the specifics of the data.

NOTE:  As of April 24, North Dakota has banned abortion.  

From 9 am – 2 pm E.T. today, share with us your responses online for the four questions.  We have added a suggestion to the “What do you notice?” question.  If your notice includes a claim, please back it up with evidence that supports your claim.  

By Friday morning, we’ll reveal the free link to the online article that included these graphs.  

You can get a link to your response to share with your teacher and classmates by clicking the response box below your response “Email me when my comment is published.”  

The last release and moderation for the school year will be Wednesday, May 5.  

The topic – baseball!

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |

raw_comment:
After looking at the graph the first thing that I notice is that a lot of the states that have restricted or banned or will soon likely be restricting or banning abortion are located on the southern half of the graph. The majority of these orange sections also make up a large part of the stats that already mandated the stress of abstinence in education. I also notice that the majority of orange states who have not stressed abstinence in the northern half of the US have pretty small populations compared to the rest. Although the orange states are most likely right-wing swinging states, it is interesting to observe that not all right-wing swinging states are orange. 

I wonder if the previous education policies have an influence on the direction of a state’s current and future abortion policies. As restriction and banning abortion is much of a recent debate and social issue, I believe that the previous stance on sex education definitely has some effect.

This relates to me because I am part of the population of women of reproductive age. Where I live in New Jersey, abstinence is promoted in sex education yet abortion is easily available and tolerated. These new abortion regulations, brought about by the Roe v Wade decision, may persuade individuals to relocate to states that allow abortion.

“Location by Chance, Babies by Choice?”

raw_comment:
1) What do you notice?
I notice that the graph is shaped like the country map, showing how states close to each other tend to be similar (states in the south mostly stressing abstinence and abortion being or soon to be restricted/banned, while the north has abortion more widely available). While mostly concentrated in middle america/south, abstinence is stressed in a majority of states. However, a majority of states do have abortion availability. Furthermore, I noticed no relation between the number of women of reproductive age and whether abortion is largely available or not. California and Texas have the largest of these numbers and yet California doesn't stress abstinence and has available abortions, while the opposite is to be said about Texas.
2) What do you wonder?
I wonder how this graph will change over the years, as theres a seemingly more pro-abortion/pro-choice stance amongst youth who in coming years will be voting, which may lead to a big shift in the regulations many states currently hold.
3) How does this relate to you and your community?
This relates to my community because in recent years there has been a strong push in legislation against the autonomy of those who can give birth, and the urgency to give people the right to control their own bodies is something I see in those around me, as we are all affected by the decisions our lawmakers are creating. 
4) My headline would be "The Ties Between Sex Education and Abortion Legislation"

raw_comment:
1) In the northeast and California, where liberals tend to reside, there is a conspicuous lack of any form of abstinence or abortion regulation. There is a greater number of states that have limitations that also require abstinence to be taught in schools, and the majority of these states are from the South or the Midwest, indicating that abortion laws are divided along party lines, with more conservative states condemning abortion.

2) I'm curious why certain states, although prohibiting abortion, do not incorporate abstinence in their curricula. I also wonder how strongly abstinence is emphasized in these jurisdictions' daily instruction. What impact does the culture of a state or region have in the decision?

3) This is relevant to my community since, where I live in New Jersey, abstinence is promoted in sex education yet abortion is easily available and tolerated. Abortion is a crucial procedure in instances where a child is unexpected and the parents are unprepared for parenting; it is a technique that allows women to make use of their bodies as they see fit. These new abortion regulations, brought about by the Roe v Wade decision, may persuade individuals to relocate to states that allow abortion.

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |
raw_comment:
What do you notice?
The first thing that I noticed is that there are much more states where abstinence is required to be stressed and abortion is heavily restricted or banned or soon likely to be. I also notice that the graph is organized like a map, and these boxed orange states are mostly in the south of the United States. 

What do you wonder?
I wonder which states have proper sex education. I would also wonder if each of the states is Republican or Democratic. 


What impact does this have on you and your community?
This relates to me since I am a woman who lives in the United States. I am fortunate enough to have had a proper sex education and abortion is available to me. However, for many women, this is not the case, and it is important to me that abortion and sex education are available everywhere with no bias. 

What is a catchy headline that captures “what’s going on this graph”?
“Abstinence or Availability?”

raw_comment:
Something I notice is that there appears to be an association between the states that require abstinence tp be stressed in sex education and the states where abortion is banned or heavily restricted. The graph supports this claim because almost all of the states where abortion is restricted teach abstinence, whereas most of the states where abortion is largely available do not stress abstinence.
Something I’m left wondering is whether the legal status of abortion in a given state is associated with the proportion of women of reproductive age (relative to the state’s total population). This visual doesn’t answer that question because only the number of women of reproductive age is shown, not the proportion.
This relates to me as a fellow woman of reproductive age, and someone who is invested in wanting all options available to me and other women. 
Something I would title this graph is “Atlas of Abortion and Abstinence in America.”

raw_comment:
At first, I wondered why they decided to format this graph geographically. I was a little confused because it looked like a wall of post it notes scattered around and thought that there could be a better format for this data. Then I realized how majority of states that are threatening to ban or already have harsh abortion laws are in one geographic area which was a nice take away from the graph. 

I think it was really important to include the population parameters of women at reproductive age because those are the people who will be most greatly affected. Additionally, I'm really in awe at how much data this graph is able to convey especially with the "stressed in sex education" aspect. I wonder how this was decided on/measured. An addition I would make to this graph if it would keep it's clarity is what kind of sex education is stressed. Some states still heavily push abstinence as acceptable sex education compared to others that do a more holistic approach. I think that would be very interesting to see against the other data being visualized. 

As a woman and an American, this graph is really scary to me. It shows me the magnitude of women affected by these policy changes. 

I think a good headline for this graph would be "The United States: Still Defending Reproductive Rights in 2023"

raw_comment:
After looking at the graph the first thing that I notice is that a lot of the states who make are have restricted or banned, or will soon likely be restricting or banning abortion are located on the southern half of the graph. The majority of these orange sections also make up a large part of the stats that already mandated the stress of abstinence in education. I also notice that the majority of orange states who have not stressed abstinence are in the northern half of us and have quite small populations compared to the rest. Although it appears that the orange states are most likely right-wing swinging states, it is interesting to observe that not all right-wing swinging states are orange. There are a lot of things going on in this graph, however, without much more than infographic data it is a bit tricky to notice any specific trends. 

I wonder if the previous education policies have an influence on the direction of a state’s current and future abortion policies. As restriction and banning abortion is much of a recent debate and social issue, I believe that the previous stance on sex education definitely has some effect. Sometimes I feel that this issue is much more on control and politics than anything.

I live in a very liberal state with an aligning political view. I think for us to become a better society for all it is important that everyone is not just heard but understood. Women should have the choice to make and not have it made for them. 

A Dividing Time

It also is clear that there is no sort of association between the size of the state and viewpoint on abortion. This is because there are states with large populations for and against abortion. This holds for small states as well. I wonder how this graph will change in the next 5-20 years and whether there will be drastic changes in both population and viewpoint. 
This decision is important because it impacts women's rights in the United States and the state of California.

A title could be "The United States is not united on Abortion and teaching abstinence"

raw_comment:
There is a square representing each state in the United States (US), in the shape of a map of the United States. There are two color choices, (orange and gray) and two border options (border and no border) for a total of four options. Of the three states with the largest amount of women of reproductive age, California has abortions largely available while Texas and Florida are heavily restricted and abstinence is required to be stressed. The states in the southern part of the US almost entirely are restricted and require abstinence education. As we travel north, there are more states that have abortions, but they also require abstinence education. 
I wonder what the breakdown in each state is. When these choices were decided, do the smaller counties also agree with what is on the map? I also wonder when this data was collected, as well as who/what the data is from. 
As a woman of reproductive age in California, I have been taught the ways to practice safe sex. Abortion has always been an option for me, but as I have gotten older there have been many news stories from around the country regarding other women’s rights for abortions. 
I would call this: "America Boxed Into Having Children"

raw_comment:
I like how the graph is organized geographically, so you could clearly, so you can see how location could affect abortion availability and if abstinence is required to be stressed. The main thing I noticed is that most states that may restrict abortion are in the South and many of them do require abstinence to be stressed in sex education. Only four states where abortion is/will be heavily restricted don't stress abstinence. There also doesn't appear to be a correlation between the number of women of reproductive age and abortion accessibility.

One thing I wonder about is how many abortions each state had before the ban. Since the graph indicates abstinence emphasis through the dark outline, I'm curious if that leads to a lower proportion of women needing an abortion and how effective it is in these states. 

This somewhat relates to me as a college student. Learning sex education and practicing safe sex is a very important topic, especially as a young adult. 

A headline I would make for this graph would be "Abortion and Abstinence in America"

Looking at the graph, I notice that abortion is either banned or restricted in nearly all of the states where abstinence is stressed in sex education. However, something that surprised me was how many states require abstinence to be stressed. I wonder what is the driving factor in abstinence being advocated at such a high frequency, as over half of the states have this policy implemented which isn't what I expected. I suspect certain religious ideologies to be a key reason why though. I think that this has a negative impact on the community, as this directly affects over half of the nation in terms women having difficulty getting the access they need, and/or proper sex education not being enforced.

I found out that most states that restrict abortion also stressed the importance of abstinence in sex education. These Conservative politicians believe that teen pregnancy will less likely to happen when abstinence is promoted.

Many female college students' future careers will be greatly affected if they were not being taught the importance of safe sex and do not have access to abortion.

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |

I wonder how many of these states allow or even mandate sex education in the first place, whether it is medically accurate, and whether that is related to this visualization. I think that the format of the visualization makes it difficult to understand immediately.  I wonder as to the proportion of women to men in positions of power who are making these decisions and mandates. 

This relates to me as a woman of reproductive age in the United States. Knowing my rights in each state is very important.

An informative title might be "The United States not so united when it comes to abortion access and abstinence policies?"

raw_comment:
In the graph, I noticed that many of the more southern states have tighter restrictions regarding abortion. The east coast gives more freedom to those seeking abortions, along with some of the more western states. It is interesting that states where abortion is largely available tend to not be required to stress abstinence in sex education, along with the opposing trend for states with bans/restrictions on abortion.
This graph certainly makes me wonder the breakdown of religious ideologies are per state. Common religious practices in the United States such as Christianity are more widespread in the South, and I wonder if there is a connection to Christian practices with an emphasis on abstinence and minimizing abortion.
As a college student from California, this certainly relates to my community as women around my age may have to face this significant problem in their lives. While the women in my state do have access to abortion, I believe there may be a higher level of sexual promiscuity due to this set up.
A catchy title may be: The Blocks That Divide Us: Connections Between Abstinence Emphasis and Abortion Approval

raw_comment:
What do you notice? If your notice makes a claim, where in the graph is the evidence to support it?
I notice that the majority of states where abortion is heavily restricted or banned require abstinence to be stressed in education. In total, only 28 states require this. Of those states, 17 states heavily restrict or ban abortion.


What do you wonder?
I wonder how the “size” of states, or the number of women of reproductive age, change as new abortion laws are passed. Will states that restrict or ban abortion become smaller? Will neighboring states become larger?


How does this relate to you and your community?
This relates to me because I am part of the population of women of reproductive age. As a college student, I personally don’t think this would be the best time for me to have a child. I wouldn’t be able to financially support it or be able to manage time for school. As a result, if I became pregnant for whatever reason, I would personally want the option to not have it.


What’s going on in this graph? Create a catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea.
This graph shows the distribution of women of reproductive age across the United States. It uses color and outlines to display where abortion is available and where abstinence is required to be stressed in sex education. I think a catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea is “Location by Chance, Babies by Choice?”.

raw_comment:
From the graph, I noticed that easy access to abortion in the United States is much harder to find than I had originally thought.  Many states with large populations, such as Texas and Florida, have heavy restrictions on abortion, meaning many millions of women are having their rights stripped from them.  Additionally, I noticed that most of the states with abortion restrictions or bans preach abstinence and are considered "southern states", with exceptions from states such as North and South Dakota.
I wonder why there appears to be such a strong correlation between preaching abstinence and abortion restrictions.  Do the abortion restrictions directly cause this preaching?  Or is it the other way around?
This relates to my community because, as a college student, I am very close with a large number of women that fall within the demographic of this graph.  While my state has easy access to abortion, knowing that many other women like them don't makes me really think about how scary it must be to be a woman in today's America.
A headline for this article could be "Triple A: The Correlation Between Abortion Access and Preached Abstinence".

1. What do you notice? If your notice makes a claim, where in the graph is the evidence to support it?
It's pretty clear that there is a geographical trend with states and their abortion law. A lot of the southern states have restrictive abortion law, while the east and west coast seem to have abortion largely available. The evidence for this claim is the fact that the southern states are colored in orange, which represents states with restrictive abortion laws. The coastal states are light grey, which represents abortion being largely available.

2. What do you wonder?
I wonder how this graph will change overtime, right now it seems like a little more than half of the states support legal abortion. I am curious to see how this number will change overtime, and wether it will get bigger or smaller.

3. How does this relate to you and your community?
This relates to me and my community, as I am a women and am surrounded by women who were affected by the overturning of Roe v. Wade. I grew up in Idaho, and as you can see on the graph, abortion is now banned. This is a scary reality for a lot of my friends who still live there, and I feel very lucky to live somewhere that abortion is still largely available.

4. What’s going on in this graph? Create a catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea.
"Abortion Restrictions and Women of Reproductive Age: A Visual Representation of State Policies"

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |
raw_comment:
1. What do you notice? 
This graph, I noticed, discusses a hotly debated subject in America. There are 29 states that permit abortion whereas there are 21 that either strongly restrict it or have it banned or are expected to do so soon. In sex education, abstinence must be emphasized in 28 states, and 17 of those states have severe laws against abortion. The majority of states that forbid abortion are also those where sex education must emphasize abstinence. I also saw that Texas and California are the two largest states, which indicates that there are more women of reproductive age in those states. 

2. What do you wonder?
I wonder if the states that stress abstinence in sex education in their schools have lower teen pregnancy rates than those states who do not. I wonder how states that banned abortion versus those that still allow each other teach abstinence. I also wonder how politics could have affected these states.
 
3. How does this relate to you and your community? 
This is relevant to me and my neighborhood because I live in New Jersey, a state that allows abortion and mandates that sex education emphasize abstinence. Abstinence is a topic that is discussed in my area, and my school has programs that educate students about it. In our freshman year of high school for example, we observed other teenagers like us educating us on such topics as teenage pregnancy prevention. 

Headline 
Abortions vs Abstinence: Is it up to You or Your State?

raw_comment:
I notice that almost every single state that has a strong abortion law in turn has abstinence required in its curriculum. There is a noticeable lack of any type of abstinence or abortion law in the northeast, and California, where liberal people tend to live. The split between abortion laws is roughly split among party lines, with more conservative states banning abortion. 

I wonder why there are some few states who, while banning abortion, still don't include abstinence in their curriculums. It's usually put together, but in the cases of those states, I wonder what education is like. I also wonder just how enforced the inclusion of abstinence is in the day-to-day education of these states. 

Living in New Jersey, abstinence is supposed to be enforced in our curriculum, but while also living in a very left-leaning area, this supposed rule is essentially never followed due to it being seen as outdated and unrealistic to achieve. We are lucky to live in a state where a basic human medical right like abortion is protected, and where women have the right to choose what happens to their bodies. 

Yet Another Divide: How do States who handle abortion handle high school?

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |

raw_comment:
In this graph, I notice that the states that have abortion widely available are historically blue states, while the ones that do not have abortion widely available are historically red states. In addition, many of the states where abortion is banned seem to stress abstinence in sex education. Many of the states where abortion is heavily restricted seem to have more women of reproductive age, whereas the ones where abortion is less restricted have less women of reproductive age.  
I wonder what the future holds for the women who are impacted by the abortion bans where they are enforced. I am curious as to what their course of action will be if they cannot get a legal abortion in their state. Will they travel far to get an abortion? Will they unsafely attempt to perform an abortion themselves? I also want to know if there will be ample resources available for women to prevent unwanted pregnancies, like sex education and ample forms of contraceptives available.  
As a woman, I am fearful for our future, as resources we need to terminate an unwanted pregnancy may not be available when we need them. This also angers me, as I feel women have less and less control over their bodies. I know that as a college-aged woman, I would not be able to provide the appropriate amount of nurturing for a child if I were to get pregnant, so the thought of not being able to have an abortion if I needed one stresses me out. 0 
Abortion Bans Slowly Infiltrating America

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |
raw_comment:
1) Something that I initially looked at was the orange States which are colored orange.  I noticed that many of these Southern States have heavy restrictions on abortion. compared to other parts of the U.S.  However, there are still some States in the western and northern areas that have banned abortion or have heavy restrictions.  For example Utah and Wisconsin.

2) I was wondering if how the way sex education is taught will influence people's decisions in their sex life.  I was also wondering if the implementation of Abstinence in sex education will decrease pregnancy rates and potentially abortion rates.

3)  This is relevant to me because abortion is a topic that is highly talked about still today.  I feel like abortion is usually connected to younger adults who seek abortions and I'm at an age where a lot of that is prevalent.  It's not unusual to hear about abortion in my community.

4) Catchy Headline:  "Is the Restriction of Abortion Taking Over The States?"

## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |
raw_comment:
A lot more of the states preach abstinence in their sexual education than I expected. Many of the states have black outlines, but my state does not. I also see that there's a correlation between whether the state includes abstinence in its education, and whether this state is attempting to ban abortions. Almost all of the states which are orange are outlined in black.

I would like to know the amount of unwanted pregnancies of these areas, and see how it compares to both abstinence-based education and the presence of abortion. 

Being from a liberal area, I grew up thinking that a well-taught sex lesson was the standard.  Most of my educators, especially in high school, came at this with the mentality that sex is a very natural practice. But, our school also had a lot of religious students, who were exempt from this type of education, and were instead offered one by their churches, which mostly preached abstinence. A lot more of these students got pregnant accidentally compared to the students who attended the school lessons. As such, a lot more of these students had to drop out of high school, and were unable to complete their education.

For each state, the graph represents whether its sex education is based on abstinence or not, and whether that state has abortions largely available. I would title it something like “The Battle over Sex Ed and Abortion: Mapping the Relationship between Abstinence-Only Education and Limited Access to Abortions by State”

raw_comment:
1. I noticed that in all states where abortion is heavily restricted or banned, abstinence is required to be stressed, but in the majority of states where abortion is largely available, abstinence is not required to be stressed in sex education. I also noticed that all the Southern states are orange.
2. I wonder about the demographics of these states. I feel that more information on the religious demographic, overall healthcare access, and sex education levels could provide a lot of insight and allow for more conclusions to be made.
3. This relates to me and my community because as young voters we have the ability to make changes in our political system. The fact that almost half the states in the US have heavily restricted or banned a basic human right in reproductive choice is incredibly concerning and absolutely absurd.
4. The Abstinence-Abortion Connection

raw_comment:
1)I observed that the majority of states on the graph that oppose abortion rights are grouped together close to the southern region of the country, and almost all of them encourage abstinence in sex education. The majority of New England has widespread access to abortion. In general, it seems that access to abortions among women who are of reproductive age is evenly distributed across the nation. 

2)I'm curious as to why certain states have one regulation but not the other, such as Washington, which mandates sex education with an emphasis on abstinence, or West Virginia, which limits abortion but doesn't mandate abstinence teaching. Does this occurrence have a logical, historical, or cultural explanation?

3)Because unintended pregnancies can occur to anyone and having a kid is a life-changing experience, this is relevant to me and my community.

4)Only one way out: abstinence over abortion
