raw_comment:
1)  To begin with, this graph is new and modern, unlike previous graphs. It discusses a controversial and current issue that is going through America. I noticed that the graph has similarities to the political US map seen after an election. Whereas, the states usually in red are where abortion is heavily restricted/ banned/ or likely to be soon. While the states that usually vote blue allow abortions. When focusing on the states that stress abstinence in sex education, there is a mix of Republican and Democratic states. 21 states ban/restrict abortions while 29 states allow them. 

2) I wonder how the states that ban abortions teach sex education compared to those that have access to abortions. Also, how do the teen pregnancy rates differ between states that stress abstinence in sex education?  

3)  When it comes to important issues like abortions, politics always tends to intersect the topic into more arguments more troubling. It becomes a separation of the nation instead of unification. This relates to my community as I take part in my school's Teen PEP program. This program allows seniors to teach freshmen about their safety, abstinence, and sex education. It gives a chance for students both seniors and freshmen to stay informed and educated. As shown in the graph, New Jersey (where I am) is a state that allows Abortion as well as insists that abstinence is stressed in sex education. 

HEADLINE
How UNITED are the States Towards Abortions?

raw_comment:
I noticed that many historically republican American states are orange and have bold borders around their squares, meaning that abortion in the state is restricted or banned, and that abstinence is present in that state’s sex education. An example of this observation is the southern states, which are usually conservative: Texas, Louisiana, Mississippi, Florida, Alabama, and others. These southern states are represented by squares that are orange and have black borders.

I wonder if the states that stress abstinence in sex education in their schools have lower teen pregnancy rates than those states who do not. I also wonder whether states that restrict/have banned abortion are states that have much less diverse demographic in terms of race/ethnicity, political party, and/or age.

This relates to me because I am a young woman and a resident of California, so I am one of California’s “8 million women of reproductive age.” The context surrounding the information displayed in the graph has a direct connection to American women's rights on abortion, thus is directly connected to my own rights. In California, the right to abortion is accepted by many of citizens of this state, which is related to why the state is grey in the graph. On the other hand, I have heard of stories about women who travel from abortion banned/restricted states that come here to get abortions.

raw_comment:
1) Something that caught my eye with this visualization is that more than half of the states allow abortion, while less than half (about 21 if I am not mistaken) have banned/restricted it, or will likely ban it soon. What I also noticed is that of the states that require abstinence to be taught in sex ed, only 11 of them allow abortion, and the rest have banned/restricted it. 
2) I wonder why more states in our country restrict abortion. I know that this issue is a controversial topic in politics, and I am not too familiar about all the caveats. I also wonder why there are a good number of states (like 19) that don't require abstinence to be taught in sex ed, and that allow abortion.  
3) This graph and the topics associated relate to my community, as my friends that have the ability to get an abortion or not depending on the state they live in are being affected by legislation that either prevents, doesn't teach, or allows abortion (or some combination of those.) It doesn't relate to me specifically, because I am not a woman, but I know people that this does relate to very much.
4) The Abortion Crisis in America: The State You Live In Determines What You Can Do With Your Body

raw_comment:
I noticed that the graph shows how many states who oppose abortion rights are clustered near the southern part of the United States, and nearly all of them emphasize abstinence in sex education. Most of the New England Area makes abortion largely available. Overall, the country appears to be equally divided in numbers of women of reproductive age having access to abortions. 

I wonder how the correlation between states that are against access to abortions and teaching an importance in abstinence in sex education is created, perhaps because of the religious beliefs in those areas, since those that define themselves as religious tend to be against both abortion and premarital sex. Also, I wonder why the creators of this graph choose these colors for this visualization. The bright contrasting orange of the states opposing abortion against the faint gray of the supporting states makes the areas in orange stand out, which is a little disheartening as a female. 

As a young woman in the US, this graph, as I mentioned, is very disheartening. It is sad to see so many women across the country that have restricted or no access to abortion. Especially because this used to be our right as citizens, it is very startling to see how far so much of the country has moved backwards in the fight for women’s rights.

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |
raw_comment:
When I looked at this graph, my eyes were drawn to how state populations (number of women of reproductive age) were highest on the coastlines and in the south. Where these states differed, however, was in abortion access. Southern states tended to be shaded orange meaning that abortion is or will be banned, while states on the coasts tended to shaded gray meaning they have abortion available. There seems to also be a connection between the lack of abortion availability and required abstinence instruction in schools in southern states.

I wonder about states like West Virginia, Wyoming, and North Dakota where abortion is not available and abstinence education is not required. I’d be curious to see whether the rate of unplanned pregnancies is higher in these states than others around the country. I’d also be interested in learning more about why certain states believe in abstinence instruction and why others don’t as it doesn’t seem like an overly polarizing concept. 

This graph represents a topic that affects everyone - both men and women - across the country. Abortion can definitely be a polarizing issue, but there’s no denying that it’s a critical one for friends and family members. 

A potential name for this graph might be, “Abortion and Abstinence Across America.”

raw_comment:
I notice that (visually) it is clear that states with heavily restricted or banned abortions are more likely to stress abstinence in sex ed.

I wonder if the patterns have other confounding variables that cause this relationship, or if the two variables shown are the primary cause of variation. 

As a man residing in California, I am fortunate not to be directly impacted by this issue, but I am deeply concerned for my female friends in states where abortion is prohibited, as this could lead to severe repercussions for their well-being.

American Education vs Legislation

raw_comment:
1. I notice the relationship between states that stress the importance of abstinence in sex education and states that heavily restrict or ban abortion. The graphic also provides a rough geographic distribution of the states that restrict abortion and support abstinence, and the populations of each state. 
2. I wonder why there are states that have one law but not the other, such as Washington which requires abstinence centric sex education, or West Virginia which restricts abortion but doesn't require abstinence education? Is there a legal, historical, or cultural explanation to this phenomenon?
3. As a male living in California, I have the privilege of having this not affect me personally, but I have many woman friends and family in the states where abortion is banned, so this could have potential disastrous consequences for them. 
4. Abortion and Abstinence Association in America

raw_comment:
From this graphic, one can see that approximately half the states provide legal abortion, while half are currently or are expected to restrict/ban abortion access. Abortion is a polarizing issue that, as the graphic shows, divides the country. There is a heavy concentration of abortion restrictions in the southern half of the US, while the abortion access states tend to lay north. It also appears that states that restrict abortion are likely also to stress abstinence. Only 4 out of 21 of the abortion-restricting states do not stress abstinence. From an east-to-west perspective, states along the Mississippi River tend to fall in the abortion restriction access. East of the river, abortion access states seem to have a more significant presence, while west of the river, it looks pretty half and half. Im honestly shocked by how divided America is and how opinions vary so much from state to state, even across small distances. I wonder what abortion opinions would be like on a district level per state. Would the divide be seen on that kind of level as well? Personally, I felt a shift in attitude when I moved from the Bay to SLO. I find SLO to be more critical of abortion and have more complicated access to it. Knowing where one can go and where one can feel safe is vital to the well-being of humans everywhere. 

Title: Abortion and Absitince Across America

raw_comment:
1. The first thing that struck me was the sheer volume of states restricting/banning abortion. Nearly half of them are colored orange. In addition, I believe that a state in the Southern United States is more likely to restrict abortion. Based on this plot, essentially all the states in the southern U.S. were colored orange, and the grey-colored ones were concentrated around the East and West coasts. 
2. I wonder if states that are restricting or banning abortion will experience a drop in population because of those who don't want to live in a state that is restrictive. In addition, I wonder if states that stress abstinence have higher pregnancy rates since birth control/contraceptives must not be as widely available in those regions. 
3. As someone who lives in California, a state where abortion is widely available as well as contraceptives, I recognize my privilege and am grateful that we have these resources for us. However, I have friends and family who are living in the orange states and I fear for their safety and freedom. 
4. Are Abortion and Sex Education Related?

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |
raw_comment:
Abortion in Correlation of Sex Education:
Over half of the states in the country are on the verge of banning abortion. I notice that there are 21 states where abortion is heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon. I wonder why in some of the states that abortion is largely available, abstinence is required to be stressed in sex education. You would think that some of the states that have to teach abstinence, would also have more restrictions over abortion. This relates to my community because I live in Washington state where abortion still is available, however, I don't recall being taught the idea of abstinence in sex education or health in general. This graph is showing the relationships between the 50 states and their sex education courses in correlation to the teaching of abstinence.

raw_comment:
1. I've noticed that abortion is heavily restricted, banned, or is about to be banned in 21 states. In comparison, there are 29 states where abortion is widely available; however, each of these categories includes multiple states that emphasize abstinence in sex education rather than birth control options. 
2. Is there a violation when the state and church are not separated in decisions about women's health? When a fetus poses a threat to the mother's health, what impact could abortion bans have on the woman's well-being? Do states that only stress abstinence see a decrease or spike in teen pregnancies? 
3. This is relevant to me and my community because I am a woman who believes that every woman in this country should have the right to an abortion because it is incorrect for the country to generalize the reasons for abortions. I also believe that many religious beliefs influenced the Roe v. Wade decision, which is incorrect because state and church should always be kept separate in order to make the best decision for the entire nation. Birth control options should be discussed with the curriculums that emphasize on abstinence so that the younger demographic can know their options when they become sexually active and be able to be informed on the risks of becoming active such as STDs if they do not use protection.
4. Roe v. Wade: Restrictions, Bans, and Insufficient Sex Education Programs that May Pose a Threat to Women's Reproductive Health in the United States

raw_comment:
The first thing  I notices is  that  all of the southern states on the  Bible Belt were orange for restricting abortions. Additionally, almost all of the orange states also stress abstinence which makes sense to avoid unexpected pregnancies. There is definitely a divide on the best way to teach sex education as well as how to handle pregnancies once they arise. I wonder how different states teach birth control as well. Do all states encourage the use of birth control methods, or do the orange states frown upon birth control usage as well as abortions?

Recently, there has been a lot of  turmoil and debate  about abortions. It's interesting to see that a lot of that debate extends all the way to education as well.

Title: Consensus Appears to be Impossible for all Aspects of Reproduction

One of the first things I noticed is the size difference
between the east and midwest states compared to the western states. Other than California, the size of the Western states appear much smaller, such as Wyoming and Montana, than the eastern and midwest Staes, such as Illinois and Pennsylvania. Because the size depends on the number of women of reproductive age, it makes sense that the states with higher populations are bigger. Another thing I noticed is that the southern states appear to be the majority of the states where abortion is heavily restricted or banned. 

One thing I wonder is if we will even see a time where all states are grey or orange, or if there will always be a split between states when it comes to ideals about abortion.

Being from California, it appears that I am in a state that has the largest number of women of reproductive age, which makes sense because we have the highest population of any state. Abortion is also largely available in California, which I was already aware of.

raw_comment:
At first glance I was quite confused with the organization and display of this graph. The different colored and sized squares made it hard to decipher how the data was organized. After reading the short description and looking at the key at the top of the graph I quickly grasped the idea. I do appreciate the simplicity of understanding the key, but I wonder why they chose squares to represent the states. In addition, the size scale to represent the number of women of reproductive age is not the easiest to follow or look at. While not very appealing to look at, this graph does present some trends. It becomes obvious that states in the South have heavy restrictions on abortion and require abstinence to be stressed in sex education. Also, without counting, it appears that about half of the United States requires abstinence to be taught in sex education and most of the states that don’t require this have large access to abortion. 
This graph is a unique way to view this data, but definitely not the most effective. As someone who was born and raised in Oklahoma who now goes to school in California, it is neat to see the differences between the two states. I have lived and experienced polar opposite environments. 
A catchy headline for this graph might be, “Abortion in the U.S. Post Overruling Roe v. Wade.”

image_description:
## 1) Chart type
**Tile grid map / cartogram (state-by-state squares)** where each U.S. state is represented by a square sized by a population measure and colored/outlined by policy categories.

## 2) Layout techniques
- **Geographic tile layout**: states arranged in an approximate U.S. map grid (not true geographic shapes).
- **Size encoding (cartogram)**: square **area** varies by a quantitative variable.
- No faceting or stacking; single panel with a legend.

## 3) Data variables and encodings

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. state (incl. D.C.) | Categorical (nominal) | Separate square mark per state; state name text label |
| Number of women of reproductive age (per state) | Quantitative | **Area/size** of each state square (legend shows example sizes) |
| Abortion policy status (3 categories: “heavily restricted or banned, or likely will be soon”; “largely available”; “abstinence required to be stressed in sex education”) | Categorical (nominal) | **Fill color** (orange vs light gray) and **outline/stroke** (thick black outline for abstinence-required category) as indicated by legend |
raw_comment:
1. I've noticed that the states with the strictest restrictions on or outright bans on abortion are typically those where sex education emphasizes abstinence. Abortion is more accessible in the states that don't emphasize abstinence. I also observe that Texas is the only state among the three largest (California and New York) that forbids abortions and mandates abstinence education. Few states prohibit or restrict abortions and don't place a strong emphasis on abstinence. 

2. This graph has me wondering if there is any connection between the sexual education that is taught in schools and abortion. Like, is it true that there are fewer abortions and vice versa the more abstinence is taught? Or the decisions we make have less to do with what we learn in school and more to do with the environments we choose to surround ourselves with. 

3. In New Jersey abstinence is highly encouraged in school, but there is access to abortion. NJ is also in close proximity to other states with abortion access. 

4.  "Cause & Effect: Abstinence v. Abortion"

raw_comment:
The first thing that I notice is that most states that require abstinence to be stressed in sex education are also states in which abortion is heavily restricted, banned, or will likely be banned soon. There are a few noticeable exceptions, like Illinois, North Carolina, Washington, and Oregon. I also notice that most states where abortion is heavily restricted are located near the South of the United States.

I wonder how the size of each state is scaled in proportion to the number of women of reproductive age within each state. Does a state with 5 times the number of women of reproductive age compared to another state also have 5 times the area shown on the graph? How will the graph look if instead of the number of women of reproductive age, it was a proportion of women of reproductive age of the whole population of the state? Does a state where abstinence is stressed in sex education tend to make the population of that state support restrictions on abortion over time?

I live in California, where abortion is both largely available and other forms of birth control are taught alongside abstinence. With recent events, it is clear that abortion in the United States is becoming more and more heavily restricted. I hope that California continues teaching multiple forms of birth control other than abstinence in sex education and keeping abortion available for all.

raw_comment:
1. I notice that most states that restricts or bans abortion require abstinence to be stressed in sex education. Some of those states like Wyoming, South Dakota, North Dakota, and West Virginia do not. But these states also have a lower number of women of reproductive age. The second thing I noticed was the map follows a similar trend to a maps that display the wining part of the electoral college. Many, but not all, red or Republican states are planning to ban abortion. This is the same for the blue or Democratic states where abortion is widely available.

2. I wonder why abortion has a lot to do with the politics. It's easy to see in the United States the division, but I wonder why each states has their own individual reasons or values to allow or ban abortions.

3. I feel this relates to me because of the recent Roe vs. Wade being overturned last year. There were large protest and a large change to the precedent the ruling gave to freedom of speech. I remember those but remember learning about the ruling before it was overturned and how important it was to some people's value of freedom.

4. "The Importance of Abstinence: States where Abortion will be banned and Abstinence is Stressed in Sex Education"
